coredata.dc:title: Corrosion inhibition properties of expired Broclear medicine and its carbon dot as eco-friendly inhibitors for mild steel in 15% HCl
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Raj Kumar
Yadav, Mahendra
coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly, hydrophilic and economical carbon dot (CD) of expired dated Broclear tablets (EBD-CD) was synthesized by hydrothermal method and its identification as a carbon dot was confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Corrosion inhibition efficiency of expired dated Broclear (EBD) and its carbon dot (EBD-CD) was analysed by gravimetric and electrochemical methods for mild steel (MS) in 15% HCl solution. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of synthesised EBD-CD was found higher at each concentration compared to EBD. The corrosion inhibition efficiency obtained from gravimetric method for EBD-CD and EBD was 95.2% and 89.1%, respectively, at 303 K and 100 ppm concentration. The inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors increases on increasing concentration and decreases on increasing temperature. The mixed type adsorption of inhibitor molecules on MS surface is the main factor to reduce corrosion of MS. Both inhibitors obey Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The surface study of uninhibited and inhibited MS surface was performed by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis. Whereas, the composition of adsorbed inhibitor film on MS surface was analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) study.

row.entry: Citric acid based CD by imidazole

head.ce:title: Corrosion inhibition properties of expired Broclear medicine and its carbon dot as eco-friendly inhibitors for mild steel in 15% HCl

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly, hydrophilic and economical carbon dot (CD) of expired dated Broclear tablets (EBD-CD) was synthesized by hydrothermal method and its identification as a carbon dot was confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Corrosion inhibition efficiency of expired dated Broclear (EBD) and its carbon dot (EBD-CD) was analysed by gravimetric and electrochemical methods for mild steel (MS) in 15% HCl solution. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of synthesised EBD-CD was found higher at each concentration compared to EBD. The corrosion inhibition efficiency obtained from gravimetric method for EBD-CD and EBD was 95.2% and 89.1%, respectively, at 303 K and 100 ppm concentration. The inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors increases on increasing concentration and decreases on increasing temperature. The mixed type adsorption of inhibitor molecules on MS surface is the main factor to reduce corrosion of MS. Both inhibitors obey Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The surface study of uninhibited and inhibited MS surface was performed by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis. Whereas, the composition of adsorbed inhibitor film on MS surface was analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel (MS) is an inexpensive alloy with excellent mechanical properties and playing a vital role in infrastructure development. Due to various application and low cost, MS attracts the attention of people towards itself and its demand is increasing day by day as industrialization increasing. Due to low price and low maintenance cost, MS is widely used as a construction material in different fields such as bridges, buildings, railways, highways, military fields, steam boilers, poles, machinery and pipelines etc.[1–5]. But unfortunately, mild steel is highly sensitive to corrosion when it comes in contact with moisture, air, impurities and aggressive medium. In last few decades, the oil and gas industry is one of the most industrialised sectors, playing an essential role in the country’s growth. These industries are important resources of energy, and are facing serious corrosion problems during recovery and transportation through pipelines. In pipeline industries, the cause of corrosion is the thermodynamic instability of metal (due to geometrical area) and the aggressiveness of raw liquids or gas (due to contamination) transported through them [6,7]. The corrosion disturbs every aspect and reduces the life and properties of metal. It is a serious problem because its rate rapidly intensifies in an aggressive medium with time and results in loss of life and money by destroying the engineered structure. According to National Association of Corrosion Engineers (NACE) corrosion results into the economic loss of around US $2.5 trillion, this is equivalent to 3.4% of the world’s Gross Domestic Product (GDP) [8]. In fact, the economy of almost all developed and developing countries are badly affected by corrosion failures. Also, as per NACE, the cost of corrosion in India is reported as US $100 billion which is 4.2 % of its GDP [8].

In the industries, 5–28% acidic solution such as HNO3, H2SO4 and HCl solutions are generally used for the pickling, descaling, and cleaning of steel. During the acidizing process tof oil wells acid solution is poured into the well through steel pipes to open the bore channels in regular basis to increase productivity [9]. The use of acid solutions in these processes cause corrosion of steel, therefore for the mitigation of metallic corrosion, these industries usually use corrosion inhibitors along with acid solutions. Organic corrosion inhibitors (OCI) containing N, S, P, and O are reported as good corrosion inhibitors for acidic environment [10–19] but some of them are toxic in nature. In the last decade, researchers give more attention to the development of eco-friendly, efficient and low cost corrosion inhibitors. In the literature it is reported that plant extracts and drugs containing pyrimidine, thiadiazole, imadiazole, isoxazoles, thiophene, etc. act as green and efficient corrosion inhibitor [20–27]. The application of drugs as efficient and green corrosion inhibitor, it is a good option to use priceless expired drugs as corrosion inhibitor. Therefore it has been reported that use of expired medicine as a corrosion inhibitor is an innovative idea to replace conventionally toxic inhibitors [28–35]. Accordingly, the aim of this work is to examine the inhibition capability of expired Broclear drugs (EBD) and its carbon dot (EBD-CD) for mild steel in 15%HCl solution. Broclear tablet is a mixture of two medicines Acebrophylline and Acetylcysteine, commonly used to prevent and treat asthma disorder and symptoms of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). It provides relief in shortness of breath due to the formation of cough or wheeze in lungs. To increase the corrosion inhibition efficiency of EBD its carbon dot EBD-CD was prepared. The inhibition capabilities of EBD and EBD-CD were studied by weight loss and electrochemical techniques. It was experimentally found that EBD-CD has high inhibition capability than EBD. The formation of EBD-CD was confirmed by HRTEM and FTIR study and its luminescence properties was investigated by UV – vis and PL spectroscopy. The inhibition mechanism was investigated by surface study using AFM & FESEM analysis. These studies suggested the presence of protective layer of inhibitor molecules on MS surface which is responsible for corrosion inhibition of mild steel.

The expired dated Broclear drug was taken from health centre IIT (ISM), Dhanbad, India. Whereas, MS sheet was taken from Tata Steel Limited, Jamshedpur, Jharkhand, India. For the weight loss study, the MS specimens with dimension 3.0 cm × 3.5 cm × 0.1 cm were cut from the MS sheet having composition (wt.%): Cu, 0.01%; Sn, 0.01%; Si, 0.02; Ni,0.02%; P, 0.02%; Mn, 0.11%; C, 0.12% and rest was Fe. Before the weight loss study, each MS specimen was polished with 80, 100, 220, 320, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 2000, and 4000 grade SiC paper. After that, the specimens were washed with acetone followed by double distilled water at room temperature and finally dried and weighted as per recommend ASTM methods [36]. For the electrochemical studies, the same MS sheet was cut into 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm to make a working electrode and polished in the same way as in case of weight loss specimens.

An analytical grade, 37% HCl (Rankem product) was purchased from a local supplier at IIT-ISM, Dhanbad and it was diluted to 15% by doubled distilled water. While, for the inhibitor solution of 1000 ppm strength (stock solution), 1 g inhibitor powder was dissolved into 1000 mL 15% HCl solution. The inhibitor solutions of 10 – 150 ppm were prepared from the stock solution.

The hydrothermal method was used to synthesize EBD-CD. For the synthesis of EBD-CD, 50 mg of grinded expired Broclear drug (EBD) was dissolved via sonication in 50 mL distilled water and resulting solution was filtered with Whatman-42 to remove the solid dispersed particles. The obtained solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 8 h. The autoclave was then cooled to room temperature and centrifuged to remove the large dots at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and then the supernatant was collected and dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 48 h. The dialyzed solution was kept in a freeze-drying box (FD-1) for 48 h. The obtained desired solution exhibited the blue fluorescent light in UV- chamber in pure condition and the whole procedure is presented in Fig. 1 .

The particle size of synthesised EBD-CD inhibitor was recorded by HR-TEM, model: Talos F200X G2, is a 200 kV HR-TEM instrument. The functional groups of EBD-CD was recorded by the FTIR-spectrophotometer, model: spectrum 2000, made by Perkin Elmer, USA. The conjugation and fluorescent properties of EBD-CD was recorded through Epoch 2 microplate reader (UV–VIS spectroscopy) and Hitachi F-7000 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (photoluminescence spectroscopy).

ce:section.ce:label: 3.3 wt
ce:section.ce:para:
The MS specimens were dipped in a 500 mL capacity beaker containing 250 mL 15% HCl solution as per the ASTM norms [38] without and with various concentrations of EBD-CD and EBD inhibitors for the 6 h at 303 K of temperature. After that, the specimens were ejected from the solution and washed with double distilled water and Clark solution for removing the corrosion products from the surface. Specimens were immediately dried in oven and weighted. The weight loss of specimens after corrosion test were recorded and used to calculate the rate of corrosion, surface coverage by inhibitor molecule, and corrosion inhibition efficiency of inhibitor by using Equations (1), 2, & 3 [37]. The experiment was repeated triplicate at 313 K, 323 K and 333 K temperature for reproducibility and to understand the corrosion inhibition capabilities of inhibitors molecules at higher temperatures.

are the rate of corrosion in the absence and presence of inhibitor, respectively.

The ECTM is a fast, relevant, and more accurate technique to detect the corrosion behaviour of MS in a various electrolyte solution. For this study, a three electrode set corrosion cell was used. Where a MS electrode with 1 cm2 area was used as a working electrode, Pt-electrode as a countered electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as a reference electrode [39]. For each electrochemical experiment 100 mL solution was used. Electrochemical experiments were performed on CorrTest CS Studio5, model: CS350 electrochemical workstation.

The analysis of polished specimen (0.5 × 0.5 cm × 0.1 cm), uninhibited specimens and inhibited specimens were done by using FESEM, AFM and XPS to show the inhibition performance of studied inhibitors. The Change in surface morphology after immersion in 15% HCl solution in the absence and presence of EBD-CD and EBD inhibitors were examined by Field Emission-Scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM): Model supra 55 (Carl Zeiss, Germany) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM): Model Dimension Icon and Multimode 8 (Bruker Corporation). Whereas, the chemical composition of the surface of MS specimens that were immersed for 6 h in the presence of optimum concentration of EBD-CD and EBD inhibitors in 15%HCl were examined by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS): Model 5000 versa probe III (PHI) by CRF at IIT(ISM), Dhanbad.

The morphology and size of synthesised carbon dot was analysed by HRTEM imaging mode and represented in Fig. 2 .The average particle size of asymmetrically spherical shaped carbon dots that are well separated from each other was 9.26 nm.

The adsorption activity of synthesised carbon dots was investigated by UV-spectroscopy. The single adsorption band at 275 nm is ascribed π–π* transition of CC bond. Whereas the shoulder/weaker peak at 345 nm, is attributed to the n–π* transition of CO [40]. Its optical behaviour was further investigated by PL study that exhibited a significant high PL spectrum at 425 nm when excited at the wavelength of 340 nm. This high value reveals that the synthesised carbon dot has high fluorescence properties. The mechanism of PL emission has been extensively debated in published literature. In the published literature, several mechanisms have been proposed to explain the origin of PL emission from CDs. Most of the reports attributed that PL emission from CDs to surface electronic states is caused by surface functional groups. The hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine functional groups are most commonly found on CDs. These functional groups not only aid in the dispersion of CDs in aqueous media and a variety of organic solvents, but they also generate/enhance PL emissions from CDs. Here, the CD glows in the blue region when excited from 270 nm to 400 nm because the photoluminescence ranges between 415 to nearly 460 nm. These excitation-dependent PL emission peaks show red shifting as shown in Fig. 3 b. These broad excitation and emission bands could be caused by non-uniform particle sizes and functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. Particles of the same size emit when a specific wavelength of light is projected onto CDs. Particles of varying sizes emit when other wavelengths are projected. As a result, the emission is excitation dependent. These functional groups are capable of producing their own energy levels. As a result, an electron can reach the ground state through a variety of photon emission routes, resulting in excitation-dependent emission.

ce:section.ce:label: 4.2 wt
ce:section.ce:para: The magnitude of weight loss of the MS specimens after immersion in 15% HCl solution containing various concentrations of the both inhibitors (EDC-CD and EDC), were used to calculate the value of inhibition efficiency (η%), surface coverage (θ), and rate of corrosion (C R) by using Equations (1), 2, & 3. The value of inhibition efficiency and rate of corrosion are.

plotted to exhibit the visual illustrate and it is given in Table 1 and flashed in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5 and Table1 signify that the rate of corrosion diminishes with higher concentration of inhibitor, in contrast the inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors intensifies with increase in concentration of inhibitor at 303 K, 313 K, 323 K, and 333 K. The result of inhibition suggests the adsorption of inhibitor on the MS-surface, at higher concentration, higher no. of inhibitor molecules are adsorbed on MS surface. But after optimum concentration (100 ppm) there is no significant change in inhibition efficiency due to complete coverage of MS surface by inhibitor molecules. It is clear from Fig. 5 and Table 1 that corrosion inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors decreases on increasing the temperature due to desorption of adsorbed protective layer of inhibitor molecules at the surface of mild steel at higher temperature [46,47].The enhanced inhibition efficiency of EBD-CD compared to EBD could be the result of its smaller size and larger surface area. The EBD-CD is being adsorbed on the MS-surface more symmetrically compare to EBD molecules.

The plot of log C R vs. 1000/T is Arrhenius plot (Fig. 6 ) and the slop value of each plot was used to determine the activation energy of each concentration by using
E a
= - (slope) × 2.303 × R relation and tabled in Table 2 . The higher values of
E a
in the presence of inhibitors compared to the absence of inhibitor reveal retardation of metal dissolution and rate of corrosion reaction through shifting the energy barrier in an upward direction and shifting the rate of conversion of reactant to the product in a downward direction.

The slope and intercept value of each linear plot of log C R /T vs. 1000/T (Transition state plot) represented in Fig. 7 , were used to calculate the

reveals the spontaneous and endothermic nature of MS dissolution reaction in 15% HCl. Whereas, the negative to a positive shift in the value of

∗
with increase in inhibitor concentration suggests desorption of water molecule from the MS surface by inhibitors molecule in the intermediate step and increase in disorderness. Hence, in the intermediate step, inhibitor molecules displace the previously adsorbed water molecule and make a protective layer upon it [49].

The corrosion inhibition properties of an inhibitor are solely dependent on the extent of adsorption of the inhibitor on the examined surface. The higher the rate of adsorption, the higher will be the efficiency. So, to know the mechanism and strength of adsorption, adsorption isotherms such as Freundlich, Langmuir, Frumkin, Tempkin were tested and among these the values of R2 for the Langmuir isotherm was reaching more closely to unity, so it is considered as best fit adsorption isotherm [50]. Langmuir adsorption isotherm is expressed by equation (6) and its plot is represented in Fig. 8 .

Where R = 8.314 Jmol-1K−1, T was temperature (303, 313, 323, & 333 K), and the conc. of H2O in the solution was 1000 gL-1.

As reported by Table 3, the values of R2 for the Langmuir plot are near to unity concluded that the adsorption process of inhibitors follow Langmuir adsorption model. Moreover, it is also concise that all the adsorption sites of the MS surface are equally proficient in the process of adsorption of inhibitors molecule. They are adsorbed on the surface through monolayer formation and negligible repulsion or attraction between inhibitor molecules. Table 3 represents high
K
ads
values at all the experimental temperatures (303, 313, 323, & 333 K), this suggests that both the studied inhibitor have strong adsorption affinity to the MS surface. It is noteworthy from the Table 3 that
K
ads
values in the case of EBD-CD are higher compared to ECD. It reveals EBD-CD has stronger adsorption and inhibition proficiencies than EBD [52].

It is reported in literature that if the values of
Δ G
ads
is negative, the chemical process is spontaneous. In addition, if its value is less negative than −20 kJ mole-1, the nature of adsorption is physical, whereas if its value goes more negative than −40 kJ mole-1, it follows the chemical adsorption process and if the values are between −20 to −40 kJ mole-1, it signify physicochemical adsorption [53,54]. Some authors have also reported that the physical adsorption values of
Δ G
ads
are less than −40 kJ/mol, which are frequently interpreted as the formation of an adsorptive film with an electrostatic character [55–56]. The negative value of
Δ G
ads
listed in Table 3 represents the adsorption of inhibitors on the MS surface is spontaneous. The enthalpy of adsorption (

ads
o

and

It has been reported that if the calculated value of

values of EBD-CD and EBD suggest adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the MS surface. Because of the free movement of inhibitor molecules in the solution phase, the entropy of the metal-solution interface is initially high. The metal-solution interfacial system approaches a lower entropy state as the inhibitor molecules bind to the MS substrate [61,62].

The OCP study of an electrochemical system suggests the equilibrium state and the behaviour of inhibitor on MS electrode. The potential of MS electrode with respect to saturated calomel electrode (reference electrode) was recorded before the polarization study at different concentrations of inhibitors in 15% HCl for 30 min (Fig. 10 ). From Fig. 10, the higher negative potential value of corrosion for the MS electrode in 15% HCl solution indicates the higher rate of corrosion in acidic solution. Whereas the low corrosion potential value of the working electrode in presence of both inhibitor suggest comparatively low rate of corrosion and these potential values lie within the range of ± 0.085 V from the corrosion potential value in 15%HCl. The above observation signify the mixed nature of studied of inhibitors, it means inhibitors resist both anodic & cathodic sites of corrosion [63]. The shift of potential towards positive side in presence of inhibitor as compared to in absence of inhibitor suggested that both inhibitors are mixed in nature and dominant in anodic nature.

The anodic and cathodic polarization curves were recorded for the MS sample in 15% HCl corrosive medium without and with various concentration of inhibitors at 303 K and it is presented in Fig. 11 . The corrosion parameters of the Tafel plot such as anodic/cathodic slope values (

The higher

In contrast, the value of corrosion current is decreasing with increase in concentration of inhibitor, confirming that the inhibitor's presence is responsible for the decrease in metal dissolution and increase in corrosion inhibition efficiency of inhibitors. The difference in potential values between the samples without and with inhibitor is less than

The change in both anodic and cathodic slop value, confirm that inhibitor influence both anodic and cathodic corrosion reaction. It also indicate that the inhibitors are mixed type in nature [65,66].

The more detailed degree of inhibition with phase angle and impedance magnitude was exposed by the Bode plot (Fig. 14). The single peak of the Bode phase angle graph reveals the single time constant as already discussed in the Nyquist plot. Whereas, the non-linear curves of the Bode impedance magnitude plots at the lowest and highest frequency were associated with inhibitor-free medium and inhibited with the optimum concentration of inhibitor, respectively. Similarly, in the Bode phase angle plots, the lowest shoulder peak with a high phase angle value represents an inhibitor-free medium, while the highest shoulder peak having high phase angle value represents the optimum concentration of inhibitor [71,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) study
ce:section.ce:para:
The polished specimens (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm × 0.2 cm) of MS were investigated by FESEM before and after the corrosion test without and with optimum concentration of inhibitors EBD-CD & EBD in 15%HCl aggressive medium. The resulted micrographs of these MS specimens were represented in Fig. 15
.The analysis of micrograph which immersed in 15% HCl without inhibitor (Fig. 15b) reveals high deformation of MS specimens surface when compared to polished surface (Fig. 15a). Whereas, the micrographs of specimens that were exposed in the optimum concentration of EBD-CD & EBD (Fig. 15c & 15d) show much lesser deformation than Fig. 15b. This visual observation gives a clear massage and support that the inhibitors protect metallic substrate from acid solution. The more smoother surface of Fig. 15c than Fig. 15d supports that EBD-CD is a better corrosion inhibitors than EBD.

The surface of MS specimens (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm × 0.2 cm) were examined by AFM imaging to understand and represent the change in morphology, topology, and physicochemical characteristics of the MS surface before and after application of inhibitors. Therefore, three polished MS were separately dipped in 15% HCl electrolyte solution with EBD-CD and EBD corrosion inhibitors (optimum concentration) and without inhibitor (0 ppm). The outcome of high resolute 3D micrograph in terms of magnitude of surface-related parameters such as average roughness (Ra), Root mean square roughness (Rq), Maximum Roughness (Rmax) were compared and represented in Fig. 16
& Table 6
. In this investigation the roughness profile of all scanned surface follow the order as: Specimen in 15% HCl medium without inhibitor > Specimen treated with EBD > Specimen treated with EBD-CD > Surface of polished specimen.(SeeFig. 16 ).

The AFM image and their respective roughness profile of MS specimen that was immersed without inhibitor in 15% HCl, displays rough surface with large deep and pits generated due to aggressive attack by HCl solution. Whereas, the specimens that were dipped with optimum concentration of inhibitors, generated relatively less rough surface with moderate cracks and pits. The significant scarcity in the roughness values display that smoother texture of the specimens are retained in the presence of optimum concentration of EBD-CD and EBD. Hence, it can be concluded that the inhibitors EBD-CD and EBD significantly protect the MS for aggressive HCl environment.

XPS was used as a surface analytical quantitative tool to investigate the chemical composition as well as the bonding states of the inhibitors on the MS surface. The appearance of C, N, O, S, Fe, and Br elements at the MS surface indicate the adsorption of inhibitors molecule on the mild steel surface. The spectrum of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p, Fe 2p3, and Br 3d were deconvoluted to analyse their bonding with the others elements.
Absorbed EBD-CD inhibitor on the MS.

Deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p, Fe 2p3, and Br 3d for EBD-CD inhibitor is shown in Fig. 17 . The Br 3d spectra was deconvoluted into two peaks at bonding energies (BEs) 69.1 eV (Fe···Br) and 70.2 eV (CBr). In S 2p spectra, three resolved peaks at 162.2, 163.3, and 167.3 eV allotted to S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2, and S···Fe, respectively. The S 2p1/2& S 2p3/2 BEs are assigned to the creation of CS and CS bonds. The resolved peak of raw C1s spectrum was associated with BEs at 283.3, 28.6, 284.8, 286.7, and 287.2 eV related to CC/ CH, CN, CO, and CBr bonding respectively. The four peaks were resolved for N 1 s element at BEs 398.1, 398.8, 398.9, and 399.7 eV and assigned as CN, CN, NH, and N···Fe bonding, respectively. The four deconvoluted peaks of O 1 s at BEs 528.6, 530.02, 530.9, and 531.72 eV were resulted due to absorbed oxygen (air), corrosion products and inhibitor molecules. The five peaks were resolved for Fe 2p element, in which the first three peaks at BEs 708.8, 710.7, & 714.2 eV related to Fe···O, Fe2O3, & Fe2+. Whereas, the other two peaks at 722.6 and 722.3 eV related to αFe2O3/ Fe3O4 and Fe2+, respectively.
Absorbed EBD inhibitor on the MS.

Deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p, Fe 2p3, and Br 3d for EBD inhibitor is shown in Fig. 18 . In case of EBD, the deconvolution of Br 3d spectra provides two peaks at BEs 69.4 and 70.2 eV assigned to Fe···Br and CBr. The S 2p spectra reveals three peaks at 162.9, 164.2, and 167.9 eV assigned to S 2p3/2 (CS/ SH), S 2p1/2, and S···Fe, respectively. The four deconvoluted peaks for C 1 s element at BEs 284.16, 284.72, 285.7, 287.82 eV related to CC,CO/ CS, CN, and CO functional groups, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum was resolved into five peaks at BEs 398.2, 398.9, 399.4, and 401.1 eV responsible for CNC, NC, N···Fe, OCNC/HNH, and C (NH)C bonds/groups, respectively. The four deconvoluted peaks of O1s spectra at BEs 529.2, 530.9, 531.3, and 531.5 eV were resulted to the presence of inhibitor and corrosion products. Similarly, the six deconvoluted peaks of Fe 2p at 708.9, 710.4, 711.2, 714.6, 723.2, and 725.6 eV, which is attributed to the binding energies of Fe···O, Fe2O3, Fe···Nx, Fe2+, αFe2O3/ Fe3O4 and Fe2+.

The presence of polar (N, O, S, and π-electrons) functionalities within the drug's molecular structure and upon the surface of synthesised dot molecules are attributed to the corrosion inhibition capabilities of the studied inhibitors. The synthesised carbon dot (confirmed by the FTIR) and drug contain several functional groups, including hydroxyl, thiol, amino, amide, carboxyl, bromide and carbonyl, which aid in the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the MS surface. The inhibitors' electron-rich centres (S, O, N, π-electrons) can directly donate electrons to the metal surface, resulting in preferential chemisorption. While these polar functional groups can exist in protonated forms in the acid environment and interact with the Cl- ions adsorbed on the substrate surface, they are physisorption-prevalent via electrostatic force of interaction. Here, the current study shows that the inhibitor molecules exhibit predominant physisorption with the MS surface in 15% HCl. It suggests that the studied inhibitors occurred in protonated form (EBD+& EBD-CD+) and strongly interact with the MS surface and form protective layer. The visual observation of AFM and FESEM micrographs and the presence of CFe, NFe, and SFe bonds in XPS deconvoluted spectra confirm the formation of a protective layer on the MS surface Table 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of studied inhibitors with similar type reported inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibition efficiency of inhibitors studied in present investigation was compared with other similar type of inhibitors reported in literature [83–94] as shown in Table 8 and it can be seen that efficiency of studied inhibitors is higher than most of the reported inhibitors. Thus the investigated inhibitors are novel and provide value addition in corrosion inhibition studies.

Expired Broclear drug (EBD) and its carbon dot (EBD-CD) acts as efficient green corrosion inhibitor and corrosion inhibition efficiency of EBD-CD is superior than EBD as a result of its smaller size and larger surface area. FTIR, UV–VIS, Pl, HRTEM, and XPS were used to characterise the carbon dot (EBD-CD). The inhibition efficiency obtained by weight-loss study in 15% HCl for EBD-CD and EBD at 100 ppm & 303 K is 95.2% and 88.7%, respectively. The adsorption of EBD and EBD-CD on the mild steel surface followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The
Δ H
ads
values of mild steel in 15% HCl environments with inhibitors indicating that adsorption of EBD and EBD-CD followed physisorption. FESEM & AFM surface testing indicate the formation of an adsorption film, which was completely consistent with the Langmuir adsorption model. XPS studies confirmed the adsorption of inhibitor molecules at the surface of MS.

The datasets generated during and/or analysed during the current study are confidential.
